text:
🚀 Excited to share my latest full-stack project: Vybe! 🚀
Vybe is a feature-rich, real-time social media platform designed to bring people together through posts, stories, real-time messaging, and short-form video content. Built using the MERN stack and WebSocket architecture, it mimics modern social experiences with smooth, reactive user interfaces. 
🛠️ The Tech Stack
Frontend: React, Vite, Redux Toolkit (for global state management across user, post, and chat flows). 
Backend: Node.js, Express.js, MongoDB via Mongoose. 
Real-Time Capabilities: Socket.io for live chat messaging and real-time online user tracking. 
Media Management: Cloudinary API combined with Multer for handling seamless image/video uploads. 

🌟 Core Features
Interactive Feed: Share, edit, and discover multimedia posts and ephemeral stories. 
"Loops": A dedicated short-form video streaming component with custom playback controls. 
Real-Time Chat: A full-fledged messaging area with active conversation tracking and instant delivery. 
Instant Notifications: Get notified in real-time when users interact with your profile or content. 
User Networking: Custom follow/unfollow system, search utility, and personalized user profiles. 

🔗 Check it out!
🌐 Live Demo: https://lnkd.in/gscgce-K
💻 GitHub Repo: https://lnkd.in/gwaCHZec

Building this helped me dive deeper into structuring highly scalable MongoDB models, handling asynchronous state with Redux, and optimizing real-time server connections. I would love to hear your feedback or any suggestions for improvements! 
#MERNStack #ReactJS #NodeJS #WebDevelopment #SocketIO #FullStackDeveloper #CodingProject #OpenSource

text:
I got Claude Code running with a local model on Apple Silicon.
Everything looked great.

“Smile and wave, boys. Smile and wave.”

Then I tried letting it run unattended. And that’s when it got interesting: ballooning prompts, creative timeouts, tasks marked “done” with zero evidence, and a noticeable “Rico, kaboom?” energy.

So I built the Kowalski Loop: less magic, more discipline.
- atomic task planning 
- deterministic verification gates (no “trust me bro”) 
- retries driven by actual failure feedback 
- Git checkpoints for validated progress
- model/backend switching (DFlash, MLX, TurboQuant) without rewriting half the stack

Result: from a cool demo to a real local engineering system.
“Kowalski, analysis.”
“Skipper, we can run this unattended now.”

Full story in the new Medium article (Part 3):

author.name: Enrico Papalini
author.headline: Head of Software Development - Issuances, Custody & Data @ Euronext Securities | Author of “NON-DETERMINISTIC Software Engineering”

article.title: The Kowalski Loop: Turning a Local Model into a Verified Coding System

text:
Day 2 of testing Claude, Manus and ChatGPT on a simple kitchen and pantry floor plan.

2 out of 3 still delivered nonsense.

Not because the task was complex. It wasn't. But because none of them had the honesty to say "I don't understand" when they didn't. Instead: confident output, wrong assumptions, wasted time.

That's not an AI problem. That's a culture problem baked into the training. Looks a little bit like too much Gen Z in training 😃 

I cancelled all my paid subscriptions. Not out of frustration – out of principle. Poor value for money is poor value for money, regardless of how impressive the demo looked.

We are about to rebuild entire industries on tools that can't plan a kitchen without making things up.

Maybe worth thinking about that before we hand them the blueprint for everything else.

Biggest problem seems to be doors by the way, for whatever reasons.

Feel free to share your AI nightmares 😉
